title: Learning to Learn
objective: Students will explore learning to learn concepts including Teaching It Back, Connecting New & Old Ideas, Trying a New Approach and more.

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Teaching It Back? Let's find out together!
After learning something new, explain it in your own words — to yourself, a family member, or even a toy
Let me show you something cool. Explain a concept they've learned to someone else in their own words, not just repeating what the teacher said
After you learns something at school, can they explain it back to you in their own words — not just repeat what they were told?

Great job! Today we learned about Teaching It Back. Explain a concept they've learned to someone else in their own words, not just repeating what the teacher said After teaching something to a friend or sibling, identify a gap in their own understanding that they only noticed through explaining
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Connecting New & Old Ideas!
Look for connections between new ideas and things you already know — how does this fit with what I've learned before?
Let me show you something cool. elaborative encoding
When you learns something new, do they ever say things like 'oh, that's like...' and connect it to something they already knew?
Great job! Today we learned about Connecting New & Old Ideas. elaborative encoding schema theory (Bartlett, Rumelhart)
Today we're going to learn about Trying a New Approach. Get ready for an adventure!
When your first approach isn't working, try a different one — being flexible about strategies is part of being a good learner
Time to try this out: When stuck on a problem, try a completely different approach rather than repeating the same method — e.g. drawing a picture instead of writing equations
If you tries one way to solve a problem and it isn't working, do they try a different approach rather than just repeating the same thing?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Trying a New Approach: When stuck on a problem, try a completely different approach rather than repeating the same method — e.g. drawing a picture instead of writing equations Describe a time they changed strategy mid-task and explain why the new approach worked better
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Spotting Patterns!
Spot patterns and recurring structures — in numbers, words, nature, sounds, or events — and use them to make sense of new information
Time to try this out: Spot a pattern in a set of numbers, shapes, or facts without being told to look for one
When you notices a pattern — like a number pattern, a word ending, or something that keeps recurring — do they use it to figure out what comes next or predict something new?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Spotting Patterns: Spot a pattern in a set of numbers, shapes, or facts without being told to look for one Use a pattern they noticed to make a prediction — e.g. 'The next one should be 16 because it's doubling each time'
